figures.caption: Figure 1: Poisson problems on 2D semi-torus in R3. Panels (a) and (b) show three types of interior points in intrinsic coordinates (ψ1,ψ2) without and with quadratic programming, respectively: green dots satisfy both nearly diagonal dominance conditions, red crosses violate the first condition w1 < 0, and blue circles violate the second condition γ≥ 3. The quadratic optimization approach completely eliminates points with w1 > 0 while only partially eliminating those with γ< 3. Here no boundary points are shown and all points shown are interior. The degree is l = 4, the number of interior points is NI = 6240 and the number of boundary points is NB = 160.

Figure 2: Poisson problems on 2D semi-torus in R3. Comparison of Laplacian coefficients w1, . . . , wk for points away from and close to the boundary. (a) For most interior points (green dots in Fig. 1), both nearly diagonal dominance conditions (w1 < 0 and γ≥ 3) can be satisfied. However, for some points close to the boundary (red crosses in Fig. 1(a)), the first condition w1 < 0 may not be satisfied (panel (b)) for a certain range of K . After quadratic programming, w1 < 0 can be satisfied for all these points (panel (c)). For other points close to the boundary (blue circles in Figs. 1(a)(b)), the condition γ≥ 3 may not be met (panel (d)). After quadratic programming, some points can be improved (panel (e)) whereas some points remain to be γ< 3. The degree is l = 4, the number of interior points is NI = 6240 and the number of boundary points is NB = 160.
Table 1: Convergence of the mean and standard deviation of absolute errors for the selected eigenvalues on the semi-sphere and semi-torus. Here the polynomial degrees (l , lbd) = (4,4).

Figure 6: Ablation study on the necessity of the QP stabilization. Comparison between the standard RBF-FD and the proposed RBF-FD-QP method for solving the Poisson problem on the 2D semi-torus. Panels (a), (b), and (c) display the forward error (FE), the stability measured by the spectral norm of the inverse reduced matrix ∥(A′)−1∥2, and the inverse error (IE), respectively. Results from 12 independent random-sampling trials are plotted directly (without averaging or statistics) for visualization.

Figure 9: Numerical results for elliptic interface problems. (a) A sphere featuring an equatorial jump interface with a 1:10 diffusion contrast. (b) A paraboloid featuring a highly non-convex, five-fold star interface. Left column: Stable high-order convergence of the inverse error (IE). Middle column: The numerical solution, capturing the sharp discontinuities across the interfaces. Right column: Pointwise absolute error distributions. For panels (b), (c), (e) and (f), we use l = 4, lbd = 4 and N = 51200. All simulations are run with 12 independent trials, each with a set of randomly sampled data points.

Figure 5: Solution accuracy for Poisson problems on a 2D semi-torus in R3. Panels (a) and (b) display the spatial distribution of pointwise absolute errors for degrees of (l , lbd) = (3,2) and (l , lbd) = (4,4), respectively. Panels (c)–(f) illustrate the convergence of inverse errors (IEs) for l ∈ {2,3,4,5}. All simulations are run with 12 independent trials, each with a set of randomly sampled data points.

Figure 8: Numerical results for the time-dependent heat equation on surfaces. (a)–(c) Simulation on a semi-sphere using the time step ∆t = 10−3 at the final time Tend = 0.05. (d)–(f) Simulation on a helical pipe using the time step ∆t = 10−3 at the final time Tend = 0.05. The left column shows the convergence of inverse errors (IEs) over various independent trials, examining the stability of the spatial RBF-FD-QP discretization coupled with the implicit BDF4 time integration. The middle and right columns display the numerical solutions and the corresponding pointwise absolute errors, respectively, using l = 4, lbd = 4 and N = 51200. All simulations are run with 12 independent trials, each with a set of randomly sampled data points.

Figure 4: Stencil illustration and numerical consistency for boundary operators. Panel (a) shows the restricted KNN stencil. Panel (b) shows a typical distribution of weights obtained by the RBF-FD approach for most boundary points. Panels (c) and (d) compare the weights for the outward co-normal derivative without and with quadratic optimization, demonstrating the elimination of unstable weights. Panel (e) illustrates the pointwise forward error (FE) for a circular boundary of the semi-torus, while panel (f) shows the consistency across different polynomial degrees lbd, matching the expected theoretical convergence rate O(N−l/2).

Figure 3: Poisson problems on 2D semi-torus in R3. Panel (a) shows the pointwise absolute error of ∆M using our combined RBF-FD and QP approach with N = 6400 and l = 4. Panel (b) shows the consistency of FEs for different polynomial degrees. All simulations are run with 12 independent trials, each with a set of randomly sampled data points.

Figure 7: Comparison of normalized eigenfunctions. Shown are the normalized eigenfunctions calculated by the FEM reference and the proposed RBF-FD-QP approach. Top row: Modes 1 and 4 on the semi-sphere. Bottom row: Modes 1 and 8 on the semi-torus. For RBF-FD-QP, we use l = 4, lbd = 4 and N = 51200.
